task_context.description: This is a detector design and performance characterization task for high-energy physics instrumentation. The user wants to design a homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeter specifically optimized for precision electron measurements, then simulate its performance using Monte Carlo methods. The task involves selecting appropriate scintillating crystal materials, defining detector geometry (either simple rectangular blocks or projective tower arrangements), and conducting systematic performance studies. The simulation work includes firing electrons at different energies into the detector center and measuring key performance metrics including energy linearity, energy resolution, and shower containment. The task emphasizes comparing multiple material options to identify the best-performing configuration for electron detection.
task_context.goal: Design and optimize a homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeter to achieve the best possible energy resolution for precision electron measurements
constraints: Must use homogeneous design with single dense scintillating crystal as active medium
No absorber materials allowed - entire detector volume must be active scintillating material
Cannot use sampling calorimeter designs like shashlik or accordion
Must use electrons (e-) in particle gun simulations
Must shoot particles at detector center
Simulation must be tractable for GPU parallel processing

{'report': "# Performance Characterization of Homogeneous Electromagnetic Calorimeters for Precision Electron Measurements

## Abstract

This study presents a systematic Monte Carlo simulation comparison of homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeters using three scintillating crystal materials (BGO, PbWO₄, and CsI) in two geometric configurations (box and projective tower) for precision electron detection. The BGO box configuration achieved the best overall performance with an average energy re

observations.content: QUALITY GATE FAILED: deterministic physics violations (1). Extracted metrics: {'best_detector': 'BGO_box_2', 'best_average_resolution': '1.22%', 'BGO_box_stochastic_term': '0.86%/sqrt(GeV)', 'BGO_box_constant_term': '0.56%', 'BGO_box_resolution_fit_R2': '0.95', 'BGO_box_linearity_R2': '0.9999999', 'BGO_box_containment_0.5GeV': '98.99%', 'BGO_box_containment_10GeV': '99.10%', 'total_events_simulated': '300,000', 'events_per_energy_point': '10,000', 'energy_range': '0.5-10 GeV', 'hit_counts_range': '10M-470M hits', 'materials_tested': 'BGO, PbWO4, CsI', 'geometries_tested': 'box, projective_tower'}. Verifier reasoning: All 20 steps completed successfully. Six detector configurations were designed and simulated: BGO box (best performer with 1.22% average resolution), BGO projective tower, PbWO4 box, PbWO4 projective tower, CsI box, and CsI projective tower. Energy resolution fits show proper 1/sqrt(E) scaling with stochastic terms 0.35-1.48%/sqrt(GeV) and constant terms 0.51-1.17%. All configurations show >98% containment and proper hit statistics (10M-470M hits across energies). The BGO box configuration was identified as optimal with excellent linearity (R²=0.9999999) and good resolution fit quality (R²=0.95). All physics metrics are within expected ranges for homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeters.
